coredata.dc:title: Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) coated with energy down shift layer of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for enhanced current density and stability
coredata.dc:creator: Riaz, Rabia
Ali, Mumtaz
Maiyalagan, T.
Anjum, Aima Sameen
Lee, Seoyun
Ko, Min Jae
Jeong, Sung Hoon
coredata.dc:description: Utilizing ultraviolet light by using Energy Down Shift (EDS) of quantum dots is a recent approach to efficiently utilize a broader spectrum of light, for energy harvesting. Other than higher efficiency, EDS is highly promising for increasing the stability of Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSC); as it decreases the UV degradation of the cell. Previously, heavy metals-based (Cadmium etc) quantum dots are used for the purpose, which are not only toxic but also have less stability and have complicated synthesis methods. To overcome these issues, a thin coating of green emissive Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) on DSSC, as a stable and efficient EDS layer is proposed here. N-CQDs were synthesized by a scalable, green and facile one-pot hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of nitrogen source, with a production yield of 65%, and quantum yield of 70%. The readily dispersible and highly stable colloid of N-CQDs was coated on the device, and with an optimized thickness, the power conversion efficiency was increased by 10%. This increase was further confirmed by external quantum efficiency (EQE) test, i.e. N-CQDs coated device showed EQE enhancement majorly in the UV spectrum and enhanced stability in UV light was recorded. The transmittance of FTO-glass was increased due to EDS and it also has an antireflection effect, which intern produces a small increase in EQE in the visible spectrum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Energy-down shift
Photoluminescence
Facile synthesis
Dye-sensitized solar cells
Green emissive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of counter-electrode and cell assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) High-resolution SEM images of CQDs coated FTO-glass (c) TEM images of N-CQDs (d) Atomic Force Microscopy topographical image (e) wide-angle X-ray Diffraction pattern of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey peaks of N-CQDs coated solar cell, high-resolution XPS peaks of (a) Carbon (b) Nitrogen (c) oxygen.
UV–visible (a) absorbance of N-CQDs colloid (b) transmittance of N-CQDs coated FTO glass.
(a) Photo-luminescence emissions of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths, (b) N-CQDs solution under visible light, (c) N-CQDs solution under UV light.
Reflectance of bare and N-CQDs coated FTO-glass.
EQE of DSSC with and without coating (CQD X; X is number of N-CQDs coatings).
I-V curve of DSSC at different number of coatings (CQD X; X is number of N-CQDs coatings).
Summary of J-V measurements of DSSC at different coating cycles.

head.ce:title: Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) coated with energy down shift layer of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for enhanced current density and stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemical Energy Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing ultraviolet light by using Energy Down Shift (EDS) of quantum dots is a recent approach to efficiently utilize a broader spectrum of light, for energy harvesting. Other than higher efficiency, EDS is highly promising for increasing the stability of Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSC); as it decreases the UV degradation of the cell. Previously, heavy metals-based (Cadmium etc) quantum dots are used for the purpose, which are not only toxic but also have less stability and have complicated synthesis methods. To overcome these issues, a thin coating of green emissive Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) on DSSC, as a stable and efficient EDS layer is proposed here. N-CQDs were synthesized by a scalable, green and facile one-pot hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of nitrogen source, with a production yield of 65%, and quantum yield of 70%. The readily dispersible and highly stable colloid of N-CQDs was coated on the device, and with an optimized thickness, the power conversion efficiency was increased by 10%. This increase was further confirmed by external quantum efficiency (EQE) test, i.e. N-CQDs coated device showed EQE enhancement majorly in the UV spectrum and enhanced stability in UV light was recorded. The transmittance of FTO-glass was increased due to EDS and it also has an antireflection effect, which intern produces a small increase in EQE in the visible spectrum.

UV light is converted to green light by quantum dots energy down shift layer, thus enhancing the efficiency and stability of organic dye sensitized solar cell.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Photovoltaic technologies are considered a promising approach for power generation due to their ease of accessibility and zero carbon emissions. To date, numerous photovoltaic systems have been presented, for examples, silicon solar cells, organic solar cells, Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC), energy generating smart windows concentrators etc. [1–3]. However, recently a great focus is on the third-generation solar cells (especially DSSC), because of their low cost and ease of manufacturing [4,5]. Several methods have been proposed, for achieving higher efficiencies in DSSC i.e. by using novel structures of TiO2, counter-electrodes, and electrolytes etc. Despite, the given developments have enabled to achieve efficiencies as high as 11% - 12%, however, their life is unknown. Such a lack of long-term stability in DSSC is seriously impeding the commercial applications of DSSC [6].

One of the limitations of the stability is the photo-degradation of the sensitizer, especially because of Ultra-Violet (UV) light. This degradation is more pronounced in organic dyes (Indoline dyes D149 etc) which are Ru free, low cost and an eco-friendly sensitizer for DSSC [7]. In order to make practically viable and, long term stable DSSC of such dyes, their UV degradation and low efficiencies in UV must be considered [8,9]. Different strategies are employed to enhance the UV photo-excitation spectrum of the organic dyes based DSSC, e.g. by co-sensitization of two dyes [10], using UV luminescent materials modified TiO2 [11], and tandem devices [12]. Despite the given developments, efficient utilization of high energy (UV) light and high photo-degradation of the organic sensitizer in UV light are still major issues, that limit the outdoor commercial application of organic dyes. A number of methods have been applied in order to cope with the above issues, where some UV-absorbing layer applied on the surface of DSSC. However, such UV absorber films convert UV light to Infra-red, so that cannot be utilized by DSSC. As the UV portion of the light is not being utilized by DSSC; hence the performance of the cell is decreased, however, the stability is increased [13].

Energy-down-shift (EDS) materials absorb high energy photon (in particular UV light), along with simultaneous emission of low energy photon; mostly visible light. Downconverted visible light can be then absorbed efficiently by the sensitizer, without any kind of photo-degradation of the sensitizer. Hence by employing an EDS layer on DSSC, the high energy of UV is utilized in an efficient way by the organic sensitizer; thereby increasing the efficiency in specifically in the UV region, with an additional advantage of the high stability of the DSSC [14]. Fluoropolymer coating with the inclusion of luminescent materials was employed as an EDS layer on Indoline-based DSSC, and a significant enhancement in stability and performance was recorded [14]. Compared to such fluoropolymer coatings, utilizing the EDS of the quantum dots (QDs) is a facile, and scalable alternative for similar EDS applications.

Metallic quantum dots, with different dopants, are used commonly, in order to convert UV light to visible, before photo-generation [15,16]. A layer of quantum dots is usually applied on the outer surface of solar cells, and by their EDS, the performance and stability of the solar cell is increased in U.V. region (esp. from 300 to 400 nm). However, owing to the adverse environmental effects, employing heavy metals based quantum dots for cleaner energy sources is a question [17]. Furthermore, the stability of metals based quantum dots is relatively low, due to oxidation in the external environment.

Alternatively, the potential of Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) has been recognized recently, due to their substantial benefits including low production cost, increased photostability, good solubility, comparable photoluminescence quantum yield, chemical inertness, and biocompatibility [18]. Their fascinating applications in bioimaging, chemical sensing, nano-medicine, biosensing, electrocatalyst, and photocatalysts has attracted great attention for the development of facile techniques for scalable production [19–22]. In all of the above optoelectronic applications, the quantum yield (QY) is another factor that must be considered. For the purpose, different surface modifications [23,24], surface passivations [25,26], and synthesis conditions with different nitrogen content [27,28] are reported previously. Concentration and state of hetero-atom (nitrogen) significantly improves QY and Stokes shift; i.e. comparable with the Cd/Pb based QDs [29].

In this study, we report the application of such (scalable and eco-friendly) N-CQDs on the surface of DSSC, as an efficient EDS and UV protective layer. By tuning the nitrogen content in N-CQDs, the Stoke s shift of emission was red-shifted to 190 nm, and the emission (nearly 550 nm) is highly compatible with the high performance of DSSC. Modification of device with such eco-friendly and efficient QDs increase the stability and efficiency, owing to the EDS and anti-reflection effect. 10% increase in PCE, due to EDS was further confirmed by external quantum efficiency (EQE) test and UV–vis transmission test.

For the synthesis of quantum dots, the citric acid anhydrous powder, and 2,3, Di-amino-naphthalene (DAN) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA (99%) purity. All chemicals were of analytical and were used as received (without any further purification). Conductive FTO-glass (TEC 8, Pellington Co.) was used for fabrication of DSSC, whereas sensitizer dye N-719 (cis-di-isothiocyanato-bis (2, 2′-bipyridyl = 4,4'dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) bis (tetra butyl ammonium) and organic Indoline dye (D149) was purchased from Ever Light Co. Ionomer surlyn spacer sheet of 60 μm, to be used as sealant, was purchased from Solaranix, Australia. While other chemicals used in the electrolyte, including 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide (BMII), lithium iodide (LiI) and iodine (I2), 4-tert-butyl-pyridine (TBP) and anhydrous acetonitrile were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. De-ionized water with high purity was used throughout the experiment. All the glass wares were washed with dilute H2SO4 before use and washed thoroughly before use.

A simple and facile method was followed for the synthesis of N-CQDs, as reported previously [30]. Precisely, citric acid (200 mg) and DAN (200 mg) was dissolved in 100 ml of ethanol and sonicated until a clear solution was obtained. This reactant solution was then transferred to 250 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel container and was backed for 9 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the brown solution was diluted with water and subjected to dialysis for purification and separation of N-CQDs. The separation process can also be carried out by the silica column chromatography, by using hexane and ethyl-acetate mixture as eluent. Typically this process yields 55% of N-CQDs, which shows that the process can be employed at an industrial scale. Because of the highly functional surface, N-CQDs are readily dispersed to make a homogeneous colloidal solution by simple sonication or mechanical stirring. Unlike metallic quantum dots, no controlled gas environments and other complex handling procedures are required for the synthesis of N-CQDs.

Before starting the coating process the surface of DSSC was properly washed to remove adhered impurities. After drying, 0.1% colloidal solution of N-CQDs with appropriate (M-TEX) binder was applied on the surface by using the spin coating machine, at 1500 rpm for 60 s. Then, this coated cell was sintered at 80 °C for 15 min, to assure cross-linking of the binder and proper attachment of N-CQDs with the glass surface. This coating process was repeated until the optimized thickness of the layer was achieved; samples are coded accordingly i.e. CQD X, where X = 0 for bare silicon solar cell and X = 1,2,3,4 refers to the numbers of coatings.

Photo-anodes were prepared by the same method as reported in our previous works [31]. Typically, FTO glass of resistance < 8 Ω was washed with water, acetone, and ethanol. The washed glass was treated with 40 mM TiCl4 solution and then sintered to make fine blocking layer of TiO2. Afterward, the active 13 μm layer paste of 20 nm sized particles of TiO2 was applied and dried at 130 °C for 10 min. On this, 5 μm scattering layer of 50 nm sized particles of TiO2 was applied by the same doctor blade method. Both the pastes were sintered at 500 °C for 30 min while keeping the heat ramp at 5 °C/min. Finally, Post-TiCl4 treatment was carried out and then photoanodes were immersed in the dye (N-719 and Indoline dye15:85 in ethanol) for 18 h under dark conditions. Indoline based organic dyes are Ru free, but their stability and efficiencies are much low; therefore a mixture was taken for this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of counter-electrode and cell assembly
ce:section.ce:para: A two-holed, Platinised FTO was used as a counter electrode, which was manufactured by the spin coating the 5 mM H2PtCl6 solution. The final counter electrode was prepared by sintering at 450 °C for 30 min. Photoanode and counter-electrode were sandwiched by ionomer surlyn of 60 μm, using hot press machine. In this space, the liquid electrolyte was filled; and the holes were blocked using the surlyn and glass protector. Finally, before testing, the cell was masked by an apertured black tape, to avoid the overestimation of the photovoltaic performance.

The detailed morphology, size distribution and crystal lattice of N-CQDs were analyzed with transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEOL JEM 2100 F). Furthermore, attachment of N-CQDs on the surface of the glass and morphology of the coated cell was further confirmed by the atomic force microscopy (AFM), Park Systems XE-70, in a taping mode. Wide angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) was performed to study the graphitic planes of N-CQDs, with Rigaku Denki X-ray generator (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) having the Cu Kα radiation.

To study the surface functional groups of N-CQDs the X-ray Photo Spectroscopy (XPS) by using multi-scale ESCA 2000 system with an energy step size of 0.05 eV. To further analyze the surface groups and types of bonds, Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out in ATR mode, using Nicolett™ iS™ FTIR spectrophotometer from Thermo Fisher, Scientific USA.

To study the optical properties of N-CQDs at different wavelengths of light, UV–Visible spectroscopy (Shimadzu Co, Koyoto Japan) was used. Photo-Luminescence-Excitation (PLE) was further characterized to study the fluorescence behavior of quantum dots, using fluorescence spectrophotometer (SCINCO, South Korea). QY was measured by comparing integrated sum with the known standard; quinine sulfate in 0.5 M H2SO4 (54% QY). Solar cell efficiency was tested on the solar simulator (New-Port) having Xenon arc lamp of 160 W, as a light source (percent non-uniformity of irradiance; ≤±2%, Spectral match; 0.75–1.25, Temporal instability; <2%). The light intensity was calibrated with the KIER-calibrated Si-solar cell, before performing the efficiency test. Stability of cell was tested by exposing the solar cells to strong UV light, in UV chamber (Spectoronics, X1000 crosslinker) for a different time. Variation in efficiency after different time was recorded for analyzing the stability of the cell.

Circular morphology of N-CQDs was observed in the TEM image of N-CQDs (Fig. 1 (a)), where the high-resolution insert of TEM shows the single particle size of 5.5 nm. The clear surface boundaries of N-CQDs were not observed because of the amorphous surface capping and oxidized surface of the N-CQDs. This amorphous layer of the N-CQDs is because of low-temperature synthesis, else high-temperature pyrolysis makes highly crystalline N-CQDs. Size distribution of the N-CQDs observed from the TEM image shows the variation between 3 nm – 6 nm, whereas the average size of N-CQDs is around 5 nm.

N-CQDs was further analyzed by AFM characterization, whereas quantum dots sized roughness can be observed on the surface of FTO in Fig. 1(b). Height profile of N-CQDs coated glass shows the variation of height between 2 nm–5 nm, showing that the N-CQDs are composed of five to fifteen layers of graphene. AFM was tested after a single coating of N-CQDs because thicker coating causes the charging effect; as N-CQDs are electrically an insulator. The AFM image (inset of Fig. 1(b), also further confirmed that the N-CQDs are completely dispersed, and are uniformly coated on the FTO glass surface.

WAXD testing of N-CQDs coated FTO glass shows a broad peak around 23.5°, referred to (002) graphitic plane, as shown in Fig. 1 (d). The interlayer spacing calculated from WAXD peak using Scherrer equation confirms an interlayer spacing of 0.384 nm, which is in agreement with the TEM measurements. An increase in interlayer spacing as compared to (002) graphitic plane in natural graphite (0.335 nm) can be attributed to the highly functional surface of N-CQDs, as commonly observed in highly oxidized graphene QDs [32]. Furthermore, the broadness of peak confirms the small size of quantum dots synthesized [16].

FTIR (Fig. 1(d)) results confirm the functional groups rich surface of N-CQDs, where the broad peaks at wavenumber higher than 3200 cm−1 were observed because of hydroxyl and amino groups. The sharp peak around 3000 cm−1–2800 cm−1 confirms the presence of = CH and CH bonds, whereas the trailing edge of this peak is highly stretched; showing that N-CQDs are highly enriched with carboxylic acid (-O=C-H) groups. Whereas the highest intensity of peak was observed for the carbonyl group stretch, around 1660 cm−1 - 1750 cm−1, showing the presence of oxygenated functional groups. Another group of peaks at further lower wavenumber (1500 cm−1) is because of bending absorption of other functional groups (NO, CN, CO, etc). However, most prominent peaks among all bending peaks are at 1104 cm−1 and 1084 cm−1; corresponding to -NH2 bending and -CN bonds. The presence of these peaks along with amino stretch peak assures the nitrogen doping of N-CQDs.

N-CQDs coated FTO glass was further subjected to XPS characterization (Fig. 2(a)), to estimate the coating quality, nitrogen doping, and functional groups. N-CQDs have fully covered the FTO surface was confirmed by the absence of any additional peak for Si; thus showing that the facile method (spin coating) is appropriate for achieving the required uniform coating results. Elemental analysis of XPS (Fig. 2(a)) has three characteristic peaks, assigned as C1s, N1 s, and O1s; centered at a binding energy of 284 eV, 400 eV, and 532 eV respectively. The percentage concentration of nitrogen is 19%, while the concentration of C and O is 57% and 20%.

Detailed bonding of each element can be analyzed in high-resolution images of each peak, as shown in Fig. 2 (b, c, & d), where C1s shows four peaks at 284.6 eV (for CC/CC of graphene), 286.0 eV (for CNO), 288 eV (for CO), and 289 eV (for OCO). Similarly, high resolution of N1 s shows three peaks at 398.9 eV, 399.8 eV, and 401.8 eV; which can be assigned to CNC (pyridinic N), CNC (pyrrolic N), and C3-N/N-H/NH2 respectively. High-resolution spectra of O1s showed two peaks at 531 eV and 532.7 eV, which correspond to the OCO/CO and NCO respectively. From the peaks at high resolution, the complete internal bonding and surface functional groups can be understood, and also it can be concluded that N-CQDs are highly functional, having groups like amide, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid etc.

The absorbance of UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3(a)) for the N-CQDs colloid show three peaks, first sharp peak at 250–330 nm, second broad peak at 330 to 370 nm and a shoulder peak in the region (370 nm–410 nm). The first absorbance band at high energy is because of π − π* transition of the CC bond, while the second peak is due to the n-π* transitions of the carbon core. The low energy shoulder peak is referred to as CO or CN bonds, which are low energy absorbing groups. A high concentration of such groups generates additional surface states and shoulder peak [33]. The presence of nitrogen also causes a red-shift in the absorbance peak of π − π*, and n − π* transitions, because it enhances the conjugation degree in the core; owing to its electron donating nature.

The transmittance of FTO-glass was measured before and after coating, and an increase in the transmittance because of N-CQDs coating was observed, as shown in 3(b). This increase is because of EDS of the high energy light; as the high energy light has low transmission through the thick structure of FTO-glass, however, after EDS visible light can pass through easily. Also, it can also be observed that the transmittance is increased until the three coatings, whereas the further thick coating causes the parasitic self re-absorption of the light in a thicker layer of N-CQDs, thereby decreasing the transmittance. The self-reabsorption generates from the redshift the absorbance of a thicker layer of N-CQDs, because of mutual interactions of the surface dangling bonds. This red shift in the absorbance overlaps with emission, causing unwanted reabsorption, along with wastage of energy [34].

The fluorescence behavior of N-CQDs is consistent with the UV–visible absorption of the N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The maximum fluorescence was observed in the range of 350 to 370 nm i.e. in the same range as the second UV–vis absorption peak, whereas the fluorescence peak at 400 nm is relatively weaker than 360 nm. However, both emissions of EDS on different near-UV wavelengths are (shown in Fig. 4 (a)) centered in the range of 500–550 nm. The emission is excitation wavelength independent, as commonly observed in other CQDs, having band gap transitional emission. It is also interesting to note that the absorbance and emission of N-CQDs has no overlap, which is important to avoid the unnecessary self reabsorption of EDS layer.

This emission can also be seen with the naked eye by viewing samples under visible light and UV lamp of wavelength 365 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 (b, c). Green emission of N-CQDs is rendered due to band gap transitions, whereas the emission at 400 nm was observed because of surface states [30]. The fluorescence mechanism of N-CQDs can be explained on the basis of size confinement effect of conjugated carbon core, and the surface states formed by the highly functional surface [23,35]. Full-width half maximum of the emissions is broad (70 nm), which is due to the variation in the size of the N-CQDS, as observed commonly in most of carbon QDs.

To further study the interaction of light after coating N-CQDs film on DSSC, reflection properties of the top surface were studied as shown in Fig. 5 . A minor decrease in the reflection of light was observed; where the reflection is decreased linearly as the numbers of coatings are increased. Antireflection properties of N-CQDs also contribute to the increase in transmittance and current density(Jsc), but the decrease in reflection alone is not responsible for an increase in Jsc. The decrease in reflection of UV light is due to absorption and re-emission process, where the antireflection of visible light is attributed to the light trapping effect of the nano-scale rough surface [36].

The QY of N-CQDs was calculated by following the same method as described in previous studies [1]. Briefly, the area under the curve of luminescence was integrated for both sample and reference, as explained in the characterizations section. This integrated sum was plotted against the absorption to obtain a straight line with a slope M (optical density), which was then used to calculate QY according to Eq. (1).
(1)
PL − QYs = PL − QYr .
Ms Mr
.
η s
η r
2
whereas subscript “r” is for reference (dye), and "s" is for the N-CQDs, and η denotes the refractive index of solvents used respectively. Both the solutions of equal concentrations were tested, while the absorbance of both solutions must be below 0.1 in order to avoid the parasitic reabsorption of NCQDs colloid. The QY of N-CQDs was tested to be 70%, with additional advantages of facile scalable synthesis and eco-friendliness; which make N-CQDs as a potential candidate to be used for EDS layer.

In order to confirm the EDS of N-CQDs at different wavelengths, EQE of N-CQDs coated DSSC is compared with the bare DSSC, as shown in Fig. 6 . Effect of EDs was observed to be most prominent in the UV spectrum, while a minor increase of EQE in the visible spectrum was observed due to the antireflection and light trapping effect of the nano-roughened surface. Whereas in the near-IR spectrum there is no observable effect on the efficiency of the solar cell; as there is no absorption of N-CQDs in this spectrum. Percentage increase in EQE was observed to be consistent with the percentage increase in PCE.

Analyzing the factors contributing to the increase of Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) (Fig. 7(a)), it was observed that the most important factor is the increase in Jsc. This increment is due to EDS; which enables N-CQDs coated solar cell to utilize more photons for harvesting energy. The bare DSSC has Jsc; 13.5, which is increased to 15.02 after three (optimized) coatings, thereby significantly increasing Jsc. Whereas, other parameters like Open Circuit Voltage (Voc) and Fill Factor (FF) remained unchanged after coating. A very minor increase in FF can be referred to the inter-bridging of Jsc and FF; as the number of photo-generated electrons is decreased, so their series resistance is lowered. In addition, most energy of UV absorbed by the DSSC is dissipated in thermal relaxation, and hence by minimizing that portion the thermal heating and associated resistances can be avoided. These reduced resistances are mainly responsible for the minor enhancement in FF of DSSC. Variation in other parameters upon coating N-CQDs is summarized in Fig. 7 and Table 1 . This optimum coated sample and bare sample both were placed under strong UV light of 340 nm, 12 W, Phillips × 4. The PCE for CQD-0 and CQD-3 was observed to be 2.1% and 6.3% respectively, after 3 weeks, proving that the N-CQDs layer significantly impedes the photo-degradation of sensitizer.

In this work, an eco-friendly, low cost, and stable N-CQDs dots were synthesized by a facile and scalable method, with an average size distribution of 5 nm and circular morphology. Compared to heavy metals based quantum dots, N-CQDs are a low-cost and eco-friendly alternative, with comparable QY and high production yield. Due to the highly functional surface, nitrogen doping, and bandgap transitions, the emission of N-CQDs was engineered to green (500–550 nm), which is the best performance range of DSSC. By converting the UV light to green light, the PCE of DSSC was improved by 10%, which was further confirmed with EQE test. Other than higher efficiency, N-CQDs coating also improves the stability of cell against the UV degradation; thereby increasing the useful life of DSSC. The proposed N-CQDs can also be applied to another type of solar cells, UV pumped LEDs, flexible display and bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Frontier R&D Program on Center for Multiscale Energy System Research (No. 2012M3A6A7054856), Technology Development Program to Solve Climate Changes (2017M1A2A2087353), and Research Program (2018R1A2B2006708) funded by the National Research Foundation under the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea. This work is also supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20173010013200 and No. 2018201010636A).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Baek S-W, Shim J-H, Park J-G. The energy-down-shift effect of Cd(0.5)Zn(0.5)S-ZnS core-shell quantum dots on power-conversion-efficiency enhancement in silicon solar cells. Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. [Internet]. 2014;16(34):18205–10. Available from: http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25054543.
